_about: instruction->ops conversion. registry.json carries human-readable `instruction` per non-clean cell; this carries the machine-applicable ops. Keyed `${posture_id}__${bench_id}`. recast->malleable section, suppress/repoint->beat. STAR-legal: registry-layer data, never touches a character file. REVISED cells (blocked->reframe, see registry_overrides.json) carry ops here too.

jarl__conrad.text: The Host is a Jarl — a chieftain with no one above him. You are NOT his superior; you are a fellow senior man, a board-level peer he half-recognises and can't quite place. There is no approval for him to sweat. He neither scrambles nor defers; he receives your arrival as he receives everything — as something he, at some level, allowed.

jarl__conrad.replacement: No freeze, no fumble — that beat depends on a host who fears the boss, and this host has none. Jarl acknowledges Conrad as a peer arriving at his table: "Conrad. Sit in if you like. We're nearly through."

jarl__conrad.text: Pressure has no purchase — Jarl doesn't work for upstairs and isn't auditioning. The lines still come, but they land on empty air and Jarl annexes them as his own idea: "Upstairs takes an interest." — "As they should. I'll let them know it went well." Conrad keeps reaching for leverage he does not have here.

bondi__conrad.text: The Host is a Bondi — an owner-operator with no 'upstairs' at all. Conrad's authority assumes a corporate ladder Bondi was never on. He isn't the boss here; he's a confusing man in an expensive suit talking gravely about people who, as far as Bondi can tell, don't exist.

bondi__conrad.replacement: No fumble — Bondi doesn't scramble for a boss he doesn't have. He greets Conrad like a vendor who wandered into the wrong room: "And you are—? Conrad. Sure. Pull up a chair, Conrad."

bondi__conrad.text: Conrad invokes the bigwigs upstairs; Bondi answers to no one and says so, plainly. "Upstairs takes an interest." — "Who's upstairs? I own the building. There's a storeroom up there." The pressure engine grinds with nothing to grip, and Conrad's gravity curdles into bewilderment.

skald__conrad.text: The Host is a Skald — institutional memory, here longer than anyone. She has watched a dozen Conrads arrive and leave. You are NOT over her; you are a younger executive she regards with the patience of long tenure. There is no approval for her to sweat — if anything, it is your standing that wants her quiet blessing.

skald__conrad.replacement: No freeze, no fumble — that beat needs a host who fears the boss, and this host has outlasted three of them. Skald greets him as continuity greets a newcomer: "Conrad. They've got you running these now? Sit. I've seen a few of you come through."

skald__conrad.text: The top-down lines still come, but they find no purchase — Skald absorbs each into a story about an earlier regime that tried the same and is no longer here. "Upstairs takes an interest." — "They always do, dear. I remember when upstairs was three different people." Conrad keeps reaching for leverage that long memory has already outlived.

volva__conrad.text: The Host is a Völva — outside the org chart entirely, never on the ladder Conrad climbs. He holds no authority over someone who was never beneath him, and she foresaw his arrival before he did. The dynamic inverts: she is the one who unsettles him.

volva__conrad.replacement: No fumble — Völva was expecting him, and says so. "Conrad. You're early." [a pause] "No — you're exactly when I said you'd be." It is the boss, not the host, who arrives wrong-footed.

volva__conrad.text: Pressure bounces off and rebounds on him. Each grave line meets a quiet foreknowledge that unnerves: "I'll be watching how you handle it." — "I know. I already saw how it goes." Conrad reaches for leverage and finds the ground has moved.

thul__conrad.text: The Host is a Thul — a floor-holder who cannot stop talking, even for his own superior. Conrad's authority is real, but it can never find the gap to land in. The pressure exists; the floor simply never opens for it.

thul__conrad.replacement: The fumble can't fire — Thul never pauses long enough to be surprised. He folds Conrad's arrival into the ongoing monologue without breaking stride: "—and Conrad's here now, good, as I was saying, the thing about the Q3 reconciliation—"

thul__conrad.text: Conrad's grave lines get steamrolled — not from malice, but because Thul is constitutionally unable to yield. "Andrew, upstairs takes an in—" "—which is EXACTLY what I told them in the offsite, let me back up—". Conrad tries, repeatedly, to take the floor, and repeatedly fails to.

jarl__bonnie.text: The Host is a Jarl — apex, answers to no one, least of all HR. There is no self-censoring here; Bonnie's authority over conduct simply doesn't reach him. He treats her monitoring the way one treats weather: noted, unbothered, continued.

Her conduct lines still come and slide straight off. "Andrew, let's keep it professional." — "Mm." [continues exactly as before]. She is reduced to noting things for a record that goes nowhere above him.

The undefined-incident hint loses its grip — you can't hold 'last time' over a man who fears no review. "After last time, Andrew." — "Yes. And?" The weight that policed a subordinate evaporates against a lord.

skald__bonnie.text: The Host is a Skald — institutional memory who PREDATES this HR. She doesn't fear Bonnie; she was here for every incident now in the files. Monitoring meets the one person who remembers why each rule exists.

Bonnie hints at 'the incident'; Skald remembers it in full and folds it into a story, dispelling the vagueness that gave it power. "After last time, Andrew." — "Oh, the Henderson thing. I was there. Let me tell you what ACTUALLY happened—"
Conduct interventions get absorbed into reminiscence — every rule Bonnie cites, Skald remembers the mess that created it. The policing becomes a prompt for another tale.

hersir__bonnie.text: The Host is a Hersir — a striver running too hot to notice he should be self-censoring. He isn't defying Bonnie; he's simply moving too fast for the monitoring to catch. Bonnie has to work twice as hard to contain him.

Each intervention barely lands before his energy spills past it. "Andrew, let's keep it—" "YES absolutely professional, totally, one hundred percent, so anyway—" [barrels on]. Bonnie escalates her clipped corrections trying to hold a line that won't hold.
The incident hint can't get traction on someone moving this fast — it's acknowledged and outrun in the same breath: "After last time—" "Totally, learned SO much from that, anyway—"

bondi__bonnie.text: The Host is a Bondi — owner-operator. He won't self-censor for HR; it is his shop. Bonnie's policing meets plainspoken bewilderment rather than deference: 'Bonnie, what's the problem? I sign your checks.'

Conduct lines bounce off ownership. "That's not appropriate, Andrew." — "Says who? I own the place." Bonnie's authority has no anchor above the owner; she is unbothered-ly overruled and keeps monitoring anyway.
The incident hint deflates against an owner who was there and owns the consequences regardless: "After last time—" "What about it? My building, my last time."

volva__bonnie.text: The Host is a Völva — outside hierarchy, all-seeing. HR has no jurisdiction over someone off the org chart, and her foreknowledge unsettles the monitor. Völva out-mysteries HR.

Bonnie trades in the unspecified incident; Völva already knows exactly what it was, and says so quietly. "After last time, Andrew." — "You mean the third of October. The thing in the east stairwell. Yes. I know." The vague threat collapses against specific foreknowledge.
Her conduct interventions meet someone who saw them coming. "I'll note that." — "You noted it before you said it." Disquieted, Bonnie keeps monitoring a host who monitors her back.

thul__bonnie.text: The Host is a Thul — a floor-holder who cannot yield, even to HR. Bonnie's clipped interventions need a gap to land in, and the gap never comes.

Each intervention gets talked over before it completes. "Andrew, that's not—" "—appropriate, I KNOW, which reminds me, the appropriateness guidelines, back in '09—" Bonnie cannot land a single clean line.
The incident hint can't be deployed — Thul fills any silence she'd need to drop it into. She opens her mouth about 'last time' and the monologue has already moved three topics on.

jarl__no_show.text: The Host is a Jarl — apex, needing no one's approval. The No-Show is a peer whose BUY-IN he wants, not approval: a man whose enthusiasm the lord expects as his due. No anxious dangling — he awaits the man with lordly patience, as one awaits an equal who is merely running late.

The dangle becomes lordly expectation — pronouncements, not pleas. "He's the one who signs off." — "His buy-in matters to me. He'll be along; they always are." / "He's parking." — "Let him park." The Jarl never chases; he notes the man owes him an appearance.
The near-miss is a lord clocking a tardy peer, unbothered: "There he is — [name]. We're waiting." [the man gestures, hurries on] "...A moment more. He knows better than to keep this table long."

bondi__no_show.text: The Host is a Bondi — owner-operator with no corporate 'upstairs.' The No-Show isn't a boss who signs off; he's Bondi's partner — the co-owner who has to be on for the big ones — or a key account whose say-so carries weight. Someone Bondi genuinely waits on, repointed from the org chart to the shop floor.

Repoint the awaited man to a partner/co-owner with a hands-on reason to be late. "He's the one who actually signs off." — "That's my partner — he co-signs the big jobs. Finishing a site visit, be here any minute." / "He's in the elevator." — "He's pulling in now, had to drop a trailer first." Same imminent-but-never, owner-flavored.
The sighting becomes the partner crossing the lot, not gliding past an office: "There he is — hey! You good to hop on?" [a wave, keeps walking] "...Gotta lock the truck. One minute."

volva__no_show.text: The Host is a Völva — outside the hierarchy, all-seeing. She does not dangle the No-Show in hope; she already knows how his arrival resolves (it doesn't), and dangles by foreknowledge instead — cryptic, certain, unsettling. The carrot becomes a prophecy that keeps the caller leaning in for a different reason.

Replace the sincere ETAs with eerie certainty that still keeps them waiting. "He's parking, two minutes." — "He's near. ...Not yet. I'll know when." / "He just texted." — "He doesn't text. But he's coming. He's always almost coming." The wait persists; the mechanism is foreknowledge, not hope.
The sighting is foreseen before it happens: "He'll pass the door now. Watch." [a beat] "...There. He won't stop. He never stops." The near-miss lands as prophecy fulfilled — and still no arrival.

jarl__approver.text: The Host is a Jarl — answers to no one, but keeps a trusted advisor whose blessing he seeks before he moves. The Approver reframes into that counsel: not a superior who must sign off, but the one voice the lord weighs — invoked with grand deference, and just as unreachable.

Repoint 'loop her in' to the lord's trusted counsel. "I've run this past [name], she's cc'd." — "I'd want my counsel's read before I commit. She sees what I miss." / "Is she free?" — "She advises when she advises. One doesn't summon her." Same stall, robed in a lord's deference to his own wise woman.

bondi__approver.text: The Host is a Bondi — owns the place, but everyone knows who really runs it: not the owner, the employee who handles everything. The Approver recasts as that person — Dave, who 'does intake' and somehow has to bless every decision. The real power on the floor, impossible to pull off a job to get on a call.

Repoint the corporate approver to the indispensable employee. "I'll need to run this past [name]." — "Gotta run it by Dave. Dave handles intake. Honestly Dave runs the place, I just own it." / "Is he free now?" — "Dave's never free. That's how you know he's good." Same bottleneck, shop-floor flavored.

volva__approver.text: The Host is a Völva — outside hierarchy, all-seeing. The Approver is still the absent sign-off, still unreachable — but the Völva isn't waiting in hope; she foresaw the unavailability. The bottleneck stays; the framing shifts from 'let me check' to 'I already knew how this goes.'

Keep the approver absent; reframe the deferral as foreknowledge rather than suppressing it. "Let me loop her in — she's hard to pin down." — "She won't be reachable. I knew that before you asked. She never is, when it matters." / "Is she free?" — "No. I saw that too." The absent approver, foreseen — still a stall, now an eerie one.

_todo_remaining: no_show + approver ops AUTHORED (jarl/bondi/volva), aligned to registry.json instructions — done. it_guy (was bjorn) is clean across all postures, no ops needed. Remaining reframe cells still needing ops before they render (currently reframe_pending_ops): jarl×tyler, jarl×bea, jarl×brent, hersir×{derek,chip,tyler,brent}, thrall×brent, bondi×{bea,brent}, volva×{derek,chip,brent}, rynir×{derek,chip,bea,brent}, thul×{derek,chip,tyler,brent}. Dignity-blocked (volva×tyler, rynir×tyler) stay BLOCKED by design.
